name: Tajikistan (Republic of Tajikistan / Ҷумҳурии Тоҷикистон)
summary: Tajikistan is a Central Asian civil-law unitary republic whose family-law framework operates under the Family Code of the Republic of Tajikistan 1998 (Кодекси оила) — federal civil-code framework drawing on post-Soviet civil-law tradition. Parental rights and child custody are governed by Family Code arts. 61-87. The Supreme Court (Суди Олӣ) is the apex court for civil and criminal matters; the Constitutional Court (Суди конститутсионӣ) operates separate constitutional review. Family-law matters are heard at first instance in the District/City Courts. Psychology profession is regulated through the Ministry of Health and Social Protection framework. Tajikistan is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label; courts operate substantively under the child's-interests standard codified in Family Code art. 61. Tajikistan is non-Hague Convention.

statutory_framework.title: Family Code — Parental rights and custody
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal Family Code enacted post-Soviet. Arts. 61-87 govern parental rights and child custody.

statutory_framework.citation: Law on the Protection of the Rights of the Child 2015
statutory_framework.title: Law on the Protection of the Rights of the Child
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal children's rights statute aligned with UNCRC obligations.

apex_courts.name: Supreme Court (Суди Олӣ)
apex_courts.role: Apex court for civil and criminal matters.

apex_courts.name: Constitutional Court (Суди конститутсионӣ)
apex_courts.role: Constitutional Court with original jurisdiction over constitutional review.

professional_regulators.name: Ministry of Health and Social Protection, Tajikistan
professional_regulators.role: Federal regulator of health and allied health professionals including clinical psychology.

anonymisation_convention: Tajik family-court decisions are anonymised per Supreme Court practice using initials.

key_developments.title: Tajikistan independence + post-Soviet legal framework
key_developments.description: Republic of Tajikistan declared independence 9 September 1991 from Soviet Union. Pre-1991 Soviet Tajik SSR Family Code 1969 substantively retained as transitional framework pending post-Soviet codification trajectory. Foundational political-institutional moment for subsequent Family Code 1998 + Constitution 1994.

key_developments.title: Tajik Civil War begins (1992-1997) + judicial-administrative disruption
key_developments.description: Tajik Civil War 1992-1997 between government and opposition (United Tajik Opposition) — substantially disrupted post-Soviet legal-administrative consolidation. Civil-war period ended with Tajik General Agreement 27 June 1997 enabling subsequent codification work including Family Code 1998.

key_developments.title: Tajikistan ratifies UN Convention on the Rights of the Child
key_developments.description: Tajikistan ratified the UNCRC on 26 October 1993 (during civil-war period) — framing the post-Soviet family-law-reform trajectory toward best-interest-of-the-child substantive doctrine. CRC engagement subsequently expanded as primary international children's-rights-monitoring register.

key_developments.title: Constitution of the Republic of Tajikistan 1994
key_developments.description: Constitution of the Republic of Tajikistan adopted by referendum 6 November 1994 — establishing presidential republic framework with Art. 26 codifying family-protection-clauses, gender-equality, and child-protection principles. Foundational constitutional anchor for subsequent codified family-law jurisprudence.

key_developments.title: Family Code 1998 (Кодекси оила)
key_developments.description: Federal Family Code enacted 13 November 1998 post-Soviet codifying marriage, parental rights and child custody. Arts. 61-87 govern parental rights and child custody within post-Soviet civil-law tradition. Currently operative framework — among the early post-Soviet Central Asian family-code codifications.

key_developments.title: Law on Responsibility of Parents for the Upbringing of Children 2011
key_developments.description: Federal Law on the Responsibility of Parents for the Upbringing and Education of Children enacted 2011 (signed by President Emomali Rahmon) — distinctive Central Asian statute codifying parental obligations for traditional values education, religious-family-influence limitations, and substantial parental responsibilities. Operates parallel to Family Code 1998.

key_developments.title: Law on Prevention of Domestic Violence
key_developments.description: Federal Law on Prevention of Violence in the Family enacted 19 March 2013 — establishing protection mechanisms, mandatory-reporting obligations, multi-disciplinary response framework, and explicit recognition of psychological violence within the family unit. Operates parallel to Family Code 1998.

key_developments.title: Law on the Protection of the Rights of the Child
key_developments.description: Federal Law on the Protection of the Rights of the Child enacted 2015 — codifying CRC-aligned child-protection mechanisms, juvenile-justice principles, child-development standards. Operates alongside Family Code 1998 as the substantive child-welfare anchor for family-law jurisprudence.

key_developments.title: Family Code amendments + minimum-marriage-age reforms
key_developments.description: Family Code substantively amended 2017 raising minimum marriage age to 18 (eliminating prior judicial-exception framework that had permitted earlier marriages). Among the more progressive Central Asian reforms in family-law-marriage-age domain. Subsequent amendments continued addressing early-marriage practices and women's rights.

key_developments.title: Rakhmon continuing-presidency + Constitutional reform 2016 framework + Children's Rights Ombudsperson
key_developments.description: Emomali Rakhmon continuing-presidency since 1994 (30+ years) substantively distinctive Central Asian long-presidency framework. Constitutional reform 2016 framework continuing through 2023 effects. Ombudsperson institutional position addressing PA-related issues within Family Code 1998 framework. Substantively significant Central Asian institutional context affecting subsequent family-law adjudication.

key_developments.title: Supreme Court — child's-interests substantive register
key_developments.description: Supreme Court of Tajikistan continues to develop child's-interests jurisprudence under Family Code 1998 arts. 61-87 in custody disputes including allegations of one-parent obstruction of the other-parent relationship without adopting the 'parental alienation' label as a doctrinal term. Substantive analysis within post-Soviet civil-law tradition.

structural_findings: Tajikistan operates a post-Soviet civil-law framework — Family Code 1998 follows post-Soviet codification trajectory shared with Uzbekistan, Kazakhstan, Russia, Belarus within the corpus.

Tajik Civil War 1992-1997 substantially disrupted post-Soviet legal-administrative consolidation — places Tajikistan within the post-Soviet-conflict-disrupted-codification cluster (with Georgia-Abkhazia, Moldova-Transnistria, Azerbaijan-Armenia/Nagorno-Karabakh).

Law on Responsibility of Parents for the Upbringing of Children 2011 is structurally distinctive — Central Asian statute codifying parental obligations for traditional values and religious-family-influence limitations. Among Central-Asian-state-control-of-family-religious-influence cluster (with Uzbekistan, Turkmenistan).
Family Code 2017 amendments raising minimum marriage age to 18 places Tajikistan within the Central-Asian-progressive-marriage-age-reform cluster.

Non-Hague-1980-Convention status places Tajikistan in the non-Hague-Central-Asian cluster — structural distinction from Hague-acceding Kazakhstan (2013) and Uzbekistan (1999). Joins Turkmenistan, Kyrgyzstan in non-Hague-Central-Asian cluster.

Multi-layer substantive-statutory framework: 1991-Tajikistan-independence + 1992-1997-Tajik-Civil-War + 1993-UNCRC-ratification + 1994-Constitution + 1998-Family-Code + 2011-Law-on-Responsibility-of-Parents + 2013-Law-on-Prevention-of-DV + 2015-Law-on-Protection-of-Rights-of-Child + 2017-Family-Code-amendments-minimum-marriage-age + 2024-Supreme-Court-child's-interests — gradual modernisation within post-Soviet civil-law framework.

Civil-law framework without explicit Islamic-law personal-status jurisdiction distinguishes Tajikistan from MENA religious-law cluster despite Muslim-majority demography (96% Sunni-Hanafi). Pattern shared with Uzbekistan, Turkmenistan, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Azerbaijan — Central-Asian-secular-civil-law-Muslim-majority cluster.
editorial_notes: Tajikistan jurisdiction sidecar v1.1 — deepened 2026-06-08 from 2 to 10 key_developments with full independence-to-contemporary trajectory: 1991-Tajikistan-independence + 1992-1997-Tajik-Civil-War + 1993-UNCRC-ratification + 1994-Constitution + 1998-Family-Code + 2011-Law-on-Responsibility-of-Parents-for-Upbringing-Children + 2013-Law-on-Prevention-of-Domestic-Violence + 2015-Law-on-Protection-of-Rights-of-Child + 2017-Family-Code-amendments-minimum-marriage-age-18 + 2024-Supreme-Court-child's-interests.

Civil-law post-Soviet Central Asia framework (Family Code 1998 + Constitution 1994 + Law on Responsibility of Parents 2011 + Law on Prevention of DV 2013 + Law on Protection of Rights of Child 2015 + non-Hague Convention).

PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator — substantive child's-interests analysis under Family Code arts. 61-87 + Constitution Art. 26 framework without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label.

Joins Central-Asian + civil-law + post-Soviet + Central-Asian-secular-civil-law-Muslim-majority (with Uzbekistan, Turkmenistan, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Azerbaijan) + post-Soviet-conflict-disrupted-codification (with Georgia, Moldova, Azerbaijan-Armenia) + Central-Asian-state-control-of-family-religious-influence (with Uzbekistan, Turkmenistan) + non-Hague-Central-Asian (with Turkmenistan, Kyrgyzstan) clusters within the corpus.
